name: Kapre
description: The Kapre is a mythical creature in Philippine folklore, often described as a tall, dark, hairy giant that enjoys smoking tobacco. They are typically depicted sitting in large trees, particularly the balete tree, and are known for their mischievous nature. While not inherently evil, they are often blamed for causing people to become lost or disoriented in forests and rural areas. They are believed to have a strong connection to the spirit world and are sometimes seen as guardians of the natural environment. Encounters with Kapre are often attributed to a sense of unease or a feeling of being watched in wooded areas, especially at night. They are a popular subject in Philippine stories and films, representing both a source of fear and a fascinating aspect of Filipino cultural beliefs. The Kapre's presence is often signaled by the strong smell of tobacco smoke, even when no one is visibly smoking. They are also associated with causing confusion and disorientation, leading travelers astray. Despite their imposing appearance, they are sometimes considered to be lonely creatures, seeking companionship and often forming attachments to certain individuals.
subtitle: The Smoking Tree Giant
appearance: The Kapre is typically described as a towering humanoid creature, often reaching heights of 7 to 9 feet. They have dark, coarse hair covering their bodies and a dark complexion. They are often depicted with a thick beard and wild, unkempt hair. They are usually seen smoking a large tobacco pipe and their eyes are said to glow red in the dark. They are often naked or wearing only a loincloth.
habitat: Kapre are believed to inhabit large trees, particularly balete trees, but also mango and acacia trees in forests, rural areas, and even near residential areas.
behavior: Kapre are generally considered to be mischievous and playful, but can also be territorial and protective of their home. They are known to play pranks on humans, such as leading them astray in the forest or causing them to become disoriented. They are also said to have a fondness for tobacco and will sometimes steal it from humans. While not inherently evil, they can become angry if provoked or disrespected. They are also believed to form attachments to certain individuals and may even become protective of them.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Traveler
keyMyths.description: A common myth involves travelers getting lost in the forest because the Kapre is playing tricks on them, disorienting them and leading them in circles. The strong smell of tobacco is often the first sign of the Kapre's presence.

sources.author: Maximo Ramos
sources.work: The Creatures of Philippine Lower Mythology
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Philippine mythological creatures.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Philippine mythological creatures.
